There's this sweet spot When it comes to writing a script and that sweet spots that the intersection of this unbelievably Sexy vibrant like wow Way of explaining what your product is But under no circumstances does that mean you exaggerate lie or even omit the truth In other words, you know one of the mistakes I on them, you know guys know I admit when I did wrong when I was younger Right when I was writing scripts back in the days of stratton. I never would lie I never lied, but I would omit material facts That's the same thing. Maybe it's not quite as bad, but it kind of is on some level I mean I would never do it now like you want to give people the accurate picture of what you're selling, but that doesn't mean it's not unbelievably sexy and awesome So the purpose of straight line script writing is that When you get great at it or I thought even just not Good enough. It's gotta be a bit better than good enough to I'm not I'm gonna be I'm not gonna lie to you It's not about just good enough. That's not the good enough factor. Great scripts. Just can't be good enough This is the part where you want to be excellent Please don't settle for good enough. You gotta be at least above average to really get the benefit Of a world-class script now, of course, you can close without that Those other aspects of the straight line will compensate, but if you want to get to the point Where you're closing everyone who's closeable, which is really attainable with the straight line You have to have a great script That's why the module on scripting and certification Is two and a half times as long as anything you've seen so far It's so in-depth. It has so many exercises. It's to me. I spent so much of my own time And my guys my people know here. So again the the first takeaway tonight the first overarching principle is that it is the intersection of the truth well told at the highest possible level The intersection of that unbelievably sexy and awesome with Ethics and integrity at the highest level Don't sacrifice your ethics and integrity while you're doing it And I'm telling you if you follow the rules of straight line script writing you don't have to You can have the best of both worlds and this is what you feel good about every sale you close You don't over promise and under deliver You explain it in a way that connects with people And empowers them gives them the information they need in a way that they make the right decision to buy your product All right now let's Talk about things conceptually first big picture first, right? They're getting the benefit in the future, right? you do that through words and One of the the first things I do is I give you seven in the module. Anyway, but the but what what I say in rule seven Is that in the absence of a straight line script? You set yourself up for individual salespeople to go off the reservation as I say they make things up now in reality Most of the time not always but most of the time When salespeople start lying or making things up or exaggerating. It's not because they have bad intentions They're not trying to hurt the company. They're not trying to defraud a customer They simply run out of intelligent things to say That's what happens in other words They have some basic things they can say about the product right and the beginning for the first Three to five minutes. It's enough But then if the sale drags on And they're forced to go past those very basic thoughts They start getting to this point where they Have to freeform as the phrase goes they start making things up And very often the things they make up are wildly You know out of the realm of what's true And that is not good guy. I'm just telling you that's a huge problem and by the way in the same token if you write a script and you Have things in there. It's a if it's they say a company approved script And you're including things in there that are blatantly untrue The ramifications for your company. It's far beyond just that people will get lied to it's almost like you're saying It's like almost open field running your signaling To your sales force that we don't really care about our customer. Go ahead and do what you want And I will tell you this salesman will take that and they'll run with it And before you know, it's a hundred times worse than you intended Maybe you had a little germ or something in there that everyone knew wasn't you like, oh, yeah But it's harmless white lie But that harmless white lie will take root in your company's In just the whole, you know the way in which the company views the customer And it ends up in a really bad spot. I've seen it happen. I made the mistake myself. All right So please make sure that your strips are accurate. They stay ethical and with that Let's get now to some of the really good stuff one of the Most powerful things That you can do with the script Is use your script as the basis For telling a story In other words, when I I talk about the you know, I'm gonna break it down with introduction your qualifying questions Your transition Then you have this main body of the presentation. Well, the main body Of the presentation that's really where you start making your air tight logical case You're framing this case right and What typically happens with Amateurs who don't really know how to write scripts is they make this case very dry and boring It's based feature benefit feature benefit feature benefit now you have to have that In the script But what you want to do and this is just a stylistic. It's just a little hack. I have a writing Amazing strips. I always make sure That within those features and benefits. I'm telling a story About either another customer Just like them Who is in a similar situation? Who used the product got an amazing outcome? And I want that outcome to be very broad in a sense that you know, you can't and what I don't want you is changing the story every Single time you speak to someone to match What they need in other words remember you're asking qualifying questions And what I don't want is you to keep Changing the script every single time based on what the person's needs are the reason for that is that you're gonna get a chance to do that In your first and second loop So in the beginning we're framing a case here So don't keep just changing things up every it's one of the classic mistakes Is that the customer says a few things that make him a bit unique or whatever You know you get that zero in one area and you change your whole script because you think if I just laser guide My approach to this person I'll close them on the spot And don't have to go through the whole let me think about it exercise. Let me call you back That's not the way it works people by their very nature Even when they're really really certain there's this almost Desire that's been I don't know whether it's evolutionary or programmed into us for a very young age that we want to essentially say Not know but let me think about we want to negotiate we want to make it more difficult We don't want to be that laid down that sap that and that's by the way what you said that that sap that here's the prices Oh, yeah, great. I'll do it Without trying to get yourself a better deal Or hear more Or tests getting a better deal But you know whether or not you demand it I'll give you an example like today I was pitched a service for my own company, right? and someone you know, it's uh, uh, some of that specialized in google And and uh, youtube advertising, right? And I like to farm that stuff out to experts best of breed, right? So i'm farming it out to an expert and they came back with a contract and when I looked at the contract I was very busy. So I didn't really glance at it. I saw they wanted money up front not a lot $5,000 small amount, right? But typically when i'm dealing with providers in that sort of space of advertising, there is no upfront thing In other words, you get with the way the advertising business typically works if someone's buying ads for you on google or somewhere else Or facebook they're charging you a markup on your budget So if I spent $10,000 a day, they might mark it up 15 percent or 20 percent. So i'll pay them two That's how they make their money. So when I saw an upfront fee there Right of $5,000. Hmm. That doesn't you know now intuitively just so you know I said to myself, all right, it's only $5,000. It's not a big thing. You know, I have a low action threshold, right? So but i'm like not not a big deal But I just I I just wanted to test The person by saying no, you know, I don't really want to pay $5,000. I don't think it's fair Because it's simply just not the way it works. I wanted I wanted to hear What the reason why was why are you charging me $5,000 when other people aren't You get it? Just it's just and i'm an easy sell So that's what i'm doing You know, most people would have you know, if there's money exchanging hands like this See in advertisers, typically there's no money that's exchanged. You can sign a contract with someone You don't write them a check You're giving them the right to advertise for you and then they make money by marking it up ad hoc as you go, right? but My initial instinct was I want to hear the person defend their Ask basically you get it? That's very common so I don't care How great your program is how when you ask someone for the order Even if it's like mom is a very weak objection I just like you I want to see what what's it about and it turned out by the way that I didn't really understand All the services being offered and based on the services that were being offered to me the ask was appropriate You get it? But the point is that it's a knee-jerk reaction that we have That we want to say at least once let me think about let me call you back Is that your best offer whatever it might be right? So the point is is that you don't have to Keep altering the initial main body to your presentation Based on what your prospect says. Yes in some situations now. Let me be clear right in some situations in some companies some industries Yes, it's like it's such a unique thing that Based on what the customer says to you it's going to really have a massive impact on How you present a product? but More often it's not You can have sort of a generic approach of how you frame The services that you offer And then what you do is when you go into your first loop That's when you're going to nail down The specific features and benefits the specific pain resolution that you're offering someone In more detail. You're not going to do that in the main body So point is is you want that main body In the initial meeting, we know when you say great name of the product name of the name of the server It is here's what I recommend and you talk about that You should know exactly what you're going to say Before you say it and it should be pretty much the same thing every single time I want you to basically save your customization And again, not always but for the most part. I want you to save your customization For the first and second loops And by doing that you make your life so much easier Okay, so when i'm writing is what I want you to do. I want you to really think about this and I want you to think right now About what you're selling and ask yourself this question What story Can I tell About my product That's gonna be like a generic story that it fits basically everyone you're going to speak to That illustrates what you have to offer Don't just make your script dry Feature benefit feature bear, you know, watch feature benefit. Does that make sense great next feature benefit does that make sense great feature? You god, this is level above that And one of those things that takes it up a level is when you tell a story We have been And I really believe there's almost like programmed into our dna at this point That you know, we learned by the fire over hundreds and you know Tens of thousands of years of evolution stories were told by the fire and that's how people learn We learn through stories. We can relate to them emotionally and logically It's an amazing way to be interesting And a big part of this here with your scripts is they got to be interesting You know, if you can make your script, you know, you know, just so you you want them hanging on your every word Now again, I understand sometimes you're selling thing, you know, you're selling plumbing supplies How do you make it that interesting man? But the point is by using stories About how other customers were facing similar problems, you know, or or took action and bought your products how they were You know accomplish certain things It's far more powerful and just going feature benefit feature benefit So I want you whatever listen if you're not in certification Okay, you might find it's a little bit confusing But for those of you in it's very clear how you do this because I went I gave you the step by step template How you do this as you're writing your script? So that's number one Okay, it's telling stories the second big takeaway here for the main body of your presentation Is linking your product Good or service to a trustworthy figure Linking it to a trustworthy figure in other words whenever you can sell By explaining how someone who is well respected well known Some sort of brand someone that they've heard of before That goes an incredibly long way Towards essentially building, you know, we call social proof or consensus Now you probably heard me talk about Robert Cialdini before Cialdini is not a sales trainer He's a professor that studied salespeople And he came up with these, you know, basic tendencies or basic tools That all salespeople use right and one of them Is he calls social proof or consensus that we want to essentially go along with the crowd We want to be part of the mainstream Right. Well, when you're linking your product good or service to a trustworthy figure, that's precisely what you're doing That's what you're accomplishing by saying like, you know, first when you would say like, you know, listen In the financial world you're linking something to Warren Buffett or you have something on your linking to Elon Musk Or something to, um, you know, to a well respected company Something to amazon.com when you sell products on amazon almost by linking it to or apple Or the Mayo clinic for medical related stuff Or even by mentioning a famous doctor's name Even if they've never heard of the doctor by saying, you know, x y z one of the foremost heart surgeons Why do you think that when you watch tv commercials or see ads online, they'll have a doctor in a white lab coat speaking to you It's it's authority. But also it's a trustworthy figure Now to me the best trustworthy figures of all those that are really well known They have wide name recognition because what you're establishing there's a couple of things that once you're establishing trust You're establishing consensus And also there's like sort of a cool factor if people just want to go along with what's cool So it's a really really powerful thing So again, when you look at your script right now and if you have it in front of you, you know, you should take it out By the way and just ask yourself this question am I maximizing my ability To link my product To a well known or to a trustworthy figure to a figure of authority If you're not doing that You're really really missing the boat, you know, it's like what is all influence marketing based on The whole market the the reason influence is driving crazy the label right but the reason That the influencers make money the reason people pick companies pay them is because You know and they're called influencers they influence Because they're public trustworthy figures. They've developed enough followers That by the sheer weight of their followers they become their own authority figures Whether they actually are an authority or not. I mean do you really think that someone like uh, you know, um, I don't want to name names. Okay, but whatever you know a Kardashian, right? You know all respect to her, right? She's just like human being But the point is because she has you know, x trillion how many millions you have 18 million followers against them Some crazy amount of thought. So I don't waste your time and that transitions down into your Qualifying or prospecting So the introduction takes you from the first word out of your mouth And takes you down as far as when you say let me ask you some questions Let me find out more about you to see if you actually qualify, right now For those of you with certification, right, which is hopefully most of you You've already been through the prospecting module And in the prospecting module what we do is I had you go down and write all your questions out I said don't worry about the order Just write out all your questions And those are your specific questions being the questions that specifically apply to your industry And then secondly I had you then write out your big picture questions I gave you a sample of big picture questions So what you have are these big picture questions that are basically generic questions That apply to most industries, right? And then next I had what are called your specific ones that are going to be things that you must know To find out if the person really can use your product Invent it from your product Can afford your product You get it? So in other words the the ultimate Prospecting script will be a mixture of big picture questions and specific questions So what we do here is the first step of how you're doing prospecting I said I want you to write down all your questions. That was step one, right? Step two now is you've got to go through an exercise Let's say you have both you have to say there's 12 questions, which is too many by the way You don't ask that's way too many prospects. Let's say you have 12 questions, right? But how do you know which one to ask first which one to ask second, right? We go through all the rules one by one here Okay, in other words you want to go from the the overarching rule is less invasive questions To more invasive questions Big picture questions to small picture questions General questions to specific questions So you're going to follow those rules as part of it And then the second part of that also is all of this Has to occur within the context of following a logical pathway In other words when you're writing out your qualifying questions You have to be avoiding the old ping pong Like you know you ask one question or so So you know, whatever do you live in right now? And the next question you ask so what's your you know, what kind of job do you have? They say oh, how old are your kids? You're going from personal to business to where you live How as soon as you do that People know there is something off about you that you're not an expert Let me give you the perfect example I and I use this metaphor a lot When a doctor, a really well trained doctor with a great bedside manner Does it does like you know his own what's the call it? You know intelligence gathering They ask their questions in a very specific way And it starts with big picture questions first They don't just start so tell me how much is how Well, let me examine this one spot on your body I've a really great doctor will ask you like 10 or 15 questions Before you even lip skits to the point on your body that's hurting They want to know your general health first They slowly start to narrow it down to what actually is bothering you So for instance, if you're selling any sort of product that's doing making money You're doing someone's what it's financial health You start off telling you how do you stuff over all for financial health? You don't just zero in right on the spot, right? So this section is you go from watch You go from the introduction which begins with the first word out of your FHA You know Jordan Belfort going from XYZ company. How you doing today? And you say you know you and whatever your own the words are I gave you literally there's like 10 Or 12 templates by the way in the Certification if those of you don't have it you could find one or two online I've given you but it's like 10 or 12 templates Find a template that best matches Your industry and if you can't find one And you're confused then what I want you to do is come to the Q&A prepared I really is that I said this in the cert module those if you are struggling here That's what the question and answer periods of war I've never Ran into a product that I have not been able to write a great script for There's always a way to have an awesome opening. How do you introduce yourself? So the the the way you go about it before it was before I intervene, right? The first step to this is I want you to go back in time To every single presentation that you've made Every time you've introduced yourself, you know, what are all the pictures that you made? What were your five best openings? What were your five best interests? And by the way, if you're part of a company I really strongly suggest that you break up into groups of three or four people And pull together say what are your best openings? And put them all put all those paths together and they're just logically It's a painstaking process you go through which you have one by one which one's better This one throw that one out go to the next one and by process of elimination You come up with your one one two or three best openings And then you make a decision which one sounds the best Which one you know and and again Is it's as much of an art as it is a science? But the idea is don't sit down and try to write a script like And try to reinvent it from scratch go back to what you've said in the past Use all your past presentations as the basis for each of these sections One at a time. Oh my god, bro. Wow How could we already be at a point? I got five guys I got five more minutes and what I'll do just you know Since it's a little bit shorter so I'm gonna have to catch a plane So we're gonna end it in five minutes. Then next week and I'll do another I'll do a Q&A and I'll give you another 15 minutes. I I love always over delivering, right? But my point is is that don't try to write a script just like brand Okay, you went through scripting and you say oh, let me write a script based on Jordan's methodology You're using everything you've already said You understand that you're not finding new words You're taking the words that you've been using already and Formulating them Into the best order the best story possible I think there's like it's like it's like some people say they they think they have to like do something different A straight line script is not different in different words. You're saying it's just the it's the the exact perfect order for everything you've ever said in the past And the very very best outcomes that knows it's your when will you add your absolute best introduction? When will you best during your qualifying questions? Don't make up fresh qualifying questions Use the ones that you've asked all along and then You say well, is something's missing here that you of course you want to fill in and it's missing but Use the things that have already been working for you But put them in the perfect order and then Polish the words To make the word sound more sexy So maybe you know, you know, you would say that you know, this company is the best product on the market First thing listen, this is best of breeding the entire industry It's one example how I just use different words to say the same exact thing Remember the movie you know Eretin International. It's a cutting edge height tech firm out of the Midwest Oh Eretin, they're located in Nebraska They're you know, they're they're up and coming firm out in Nebraska. Which one sounds better? I'm not asking you to come up with new ideas here.
